content.body: Magnetism is a fundamental force of nature. Magnets attract certain metals (iron, nickel, cobalt) and can attract or repel other magnets. The Earth itself is a giant magnet, which is why compasses work.

Poles of a magnet:
• North pole (N) and South pole (S)
• LIKE poles repel: N↔N, S↔S
• UNLIKE poles attract: N↔S

Every magnet has BOTH poles — you cannot isolate a single pole.
If you cut a magnet, each piece becomes a complete magnet with N and S poles.

content.title: Magnetic Field Lines
content.body:
Magnetic field lines show the direction and strength of a magnetic field:
• Lines go from North pole to South pole outside the magnet
• Where lines are CLOSER TOGETHER: field is STRONGER
• Field lines never cross
• A compass needle aligns with field lines

The Earth's geographic North Pole is actually a magnetic SOUTH pole (attracts the N end of a compass).

Induced Magnetism:
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt) can be temporarily or permanently magnetised.

Iron: magnetised easily but loses magnetism quickly (soft magnetic material — good for electromagnets)
Steel: harder to magnetise but retains magnetism (hard magnetic material — good for permanent magnets)

Methods of magnetisation: stroking with a magnet, placing in a solenoid with DC current, heating and cooling in a magnetic field

content.title: Electromagnets
content.body:
An electromagnet is made by coiling wire around iron and passing electric current through it.

Strength of electromagnet increased by:
1. Increasing current through coil
2. Increasing number of turns of wire
3. Using an iron core (not air core)
4. Using soft iron (loses magnetism quickly when current stops)

Applications: electric bells, relays, electric motors, cranes in scrapyards, MRI scanners

questions.question: Like magnetic poles:
questions.explanation: Like poles (N-N or S-S) repel. Unlike poles (N-S) attract.

questions.question: If you cut a bar magnet in half, you get:
options: One N and one S pole separately
Two complete magnets each with N and S
questions.answer: Two complete magnets each with N and S
questions.explanation: You cannot isolate a magnetic pole. Each piece becomes a complete magnet with both N and S poles.

questions.question: Magnetic field lines are closest together where the field is:
questions.explanation: Closer field lines indicate a stronger magnetic field — like near the poles of a magnet.

questions.question: Iron is used for electromagnet cores (not steel) because:
options: Iron loses magnetism quickly when current stops
questions.answer: Iron loses magnetism quickly when current stops
questions.explanation: Soft iron magnetises easily and demagnetises quickly — ideal for electromagnets that need to switch on and off.

questions.question: How can you increase the strength of an electromagnet?
options: Use an iron core and increase current
questions.answer: Use an iron core and increase current
questions.explanation: More current, more turns, and a soft iron core all increase electromagnet strength.

questions.question: A compass needle points North because:
options: The Earth's geographic North is a magnetic North
Earth's geographic North is a magnetic South pole
questions.answer: Earth's geographic North is a magnetic South pole
questions.explanation: The geographic North Pole is actually a magnetic South pole — it attracts the North pole of a compass needle.

questions.question: Which material is NOT attracted to a magnet?
questions.explanation: Only ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) are attracted to magnets. Copper is non-magnetic.

questions.question: Steel is used for permanent magnets because:
options: It retains magnetism well after being magnetised
questions.answer: It retains magnetism well after being magnetised
questions.explanation: Hard magnetic materials like steel retain magnetism — good for permanent magnets.

questions.question: Magnetic field lines travel from:
options: S to N inside the magnet only
N to S outside the magnet
questions.answer: N to S outside the magnet
questions.explanation: Outside the magnet, field lines run from the North pole to the South pole.

questions.question: An MRI scanner in a hospital uses:
questions.explanation: MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) uses powerful electromagnets to create images of the inside of the body.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Thermal energy (heat) is the internal energy of a substance — the total kinetic energy of all its particles. Heat naturally flows from hotter regions to cooler regions until thermal equilibrium is reached. There are three mechanisms of heat transfer.

CONDUCTION:
• Transfer of heat through a solid without movement of the material
• Particle vibrations pass energy to neighbouring particles
• Best conductors: metals (copper, silver, aluminium)
• Poor conductors (insulators): wood, plastic, rubber, air, water, wool
• Used in: cooking pans, heat sinks in computers

CONVECTION:
• Transfer of heat through fluids (liquids and gases) by movement of the fluid itself
• Hot fluid rises (less dense), cool fluid sinks (more dense) → convection current
• Cannot occur in solids
• Examples: central heating, sea breezes, hot air balloons, ocean currents

RADIATION:
• Transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves (infrared radiation)
• Does NOT need a medium — works in vacuum (space)
• All objects emit and absorb radiation
• Dark/dull/rough surfaces: better emitters AND absorbers
• Light/shiny/smooth surfaces: poor emitters, good reflectors

content.title: Applications of Heat Transfer
content.body:
Thermos flask (vacuum flask) reduces all three:
• Vacuum between walls: no conduction or convection
• Silvered walls: reflect radiation back in

House insulation:
• Loft insulation (fibreglass): reduces conduction and convection
• Double glazing: air gap reduces conduction
• Cavity wall insulation: reduces convection currents

Cooking a radiator black: better emitter of heat into the room

questions.question: Heat transfer through a solid without particle movement is:
questions.explanation: Conduction transfers heat through solids via vibrations passed between neighbouring particles.

questions.question: Convection occurs in:
questions.explanation: Convection requires fluid flow (bulk movement of particles), so it occurs in liquids and gases only.

questions.question: Which does not need a medium for heat transfer?
questions.explanation: Radiation travels as electromagnetic waves and can cross a vacuum — this is how the Sun heats the Earth.

questions.question: Why does hot air rise?
options: Air is always hot at the top
Hot air is less dense so it is pushed up by cooler denser air
questions.answer: Hot air is less dense so it is pushed up by cooler denser air
questions.explanation: Heating makes air molecules move faster and spread out — less dense. The cooler, denser air sinks and pushes the hot air up, creating convection currents.

questions.question: A thermos flask has silvered walls to:
options: Reflect radiation and reduce heat transfer
questions.answer: Reflect radiation and reduce heat transfer
questions.explanation: Silvered (shiny) surfaces are poor emitters and reflect radiation, reducing heat transfer by radiation.

questions.question: Which surface emits the most thermal radiation?
questions.explanation: Dark, rough, dull surfaces are the best emitters (and absorbers) of thermal radiation.

questions.question: Metals are good thermal conductors because:
questions.explanation: Metals have free (delocalized) electrons that move throughout the structure, rapidly transferring thermal energy.

questions.question: In a vacuum flask, the vacuum between walls prevents:
questions.explanation: A vacuum has no particles, so conduction (needs particle contact) and convection (needs fluid movement) cannot occur.

questions.question: Sea breezes occur because:
options: Land heats up faster than sea, air rises over land, cooler sea air moves in
The wind always blows from sea to land
questions.answer: Land heats up faster than sea, air rises over land, cooler sea air moves in
questions.explanation: Land warms faster than sea. Hot air over land rises (convection), and cooler air from the sea flows in to replace it — creating a sea breeze.

questions.question: Loft insulation in houses works by:
options: Trapping air and reducing convection and conduction through the roof
questions.answer: Trapping air and reducing convection and conduction through the roof
questions.explanation: Fibreglass loft insulation traps air, which is a poor conductor. This reduces heat transfer by conduction and prevents convection through the ceiling.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Electromagnetic induction is the production of an EMF (voltage) in a conductor when the magnetic flux through it changes. This is the principle behind generators and transformers.

Faraday's Law:
EMF = −N × ΔΦ/Δt
Greater change in flux → greater EMF
More turns N → greater EMF

Ways to induce EMF:
• Move magnet into/out of coil
• Move coil in magnetic field
• Change current in nearby coil
• Change the area of coil in field

Transformers:
V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s
V_p × I_p = V_s × I_s (100% efficiency)

Step-up transformer: N_s > N_p → voltage increases
Step-down transformer: N_s < N_p → voltage decreases

content.title: Transformer Calculation
content.body:
Primary: 240V, 1200 turns. Secondary: 12V
N_s/N_p = V_s/V_p → N_s = 1200 × 12/240 = 60 turns
If primary current = 0.5 A:
I_s = V_p × I_p / V_s = 240 × 0.5 / 12 = 10 A

questions.question: Electromagnetic induction produces an EMF when...
options: Magnetic flux through a coil changes
questions.answer: Magnetic flux through a coil changes
questions.explanation: Faraday's law: EMF is induced whenever the magnetic flux through a conductor changes.

questions.question: Increasing the number of coil turns in a generator...
questions.explanation: More turns means more conductors cutting field lines → greater induced EMF (Faraday's law).

questions.question: A transformer works on...
questions.explanation: Transformers require changing magnetic flux. Only AC produces a changing current → changing flux. DC produces constant flux.

questions.question: V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s is the...
questions.explanation: The transformer ratio equation relates primary/secondary voltages to the number of turns.

questions.question: A step-up transformer has V_p=230V, N_p=100, N_s=500. V_s =
National grid transmits power at high voltage to...
questions.explanation: P_loss = I²R. High voltage = low current → much less power lost as heat in transmission cables.

questions.question: Lenz's law states the induced current opposes...
questions.explanation: Lenz's law: induced current creates its own magnetic field that opposes the original change in flux (conservation of energy).

questions.question: A transformer is 100% efficient. V_p=12V, I_p=5A, V_s=60V. I_s =
questions.explanation: Power in = Power out: 12×5 = 60 W. I_s = 60/60 = 1 A

questions.question: Generators convert...
questions.explanation: Generators use electromagnetic induction — rotating coil in magnetic field → induced EMF → electrical energy.

questions.question: Soft iron core in transformer...
options: Concentrates magnetic flux between primary and secondary
questions.answer: Concentrates magnetic flux between primary and secondary
questions.explanation: Soft iron is easily magnetised/demagnetised → it channels and concentrates changing magnetic flux efficiently.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Most materials expand when heated due to increased particle vibration. This principle has important engineering applications.

Linear expansion:
ΔL = αL₀ΔT
ΔL = change in length, α = linear expansion coefficient, L₀ = original length, ΔT = temperature change

Volume expansion:
ΔV = γV₀ΔT (γ ≈ 3α for isotropic materials)

content.title: Thermal Expansion Applications
content.body:
Bimetallic strip: two metals bonded together that expand at different rates → strips bends when heated → used in thermostats
Expansion gaps in railway lines and bridges: allow expansion in summer without buckling
Loose fitting of metal bands on wooden barrels
Rivet setting: hot rivet is inserted and cools, contracting to clamp metal plates

content.title: Anomalous Expansion of Water
content.body:
Water is unique — it expands as it COOLS from 4°C to 0°C
Maximum density at 4°C
Ice is less dense than water → floats → lakes freeze from top
This protects aquatic life in winter

questions.question: Most materials expand when heated because...
options: Particles vibrate more and take up more space
questions.answer: Particles vibrate more and take up more space
questions.explanation: Heating increases particle kinetic energy. Particles vibrate more vigorously and push each other further apart.

questions.question: Expansion gaps in railway tracks prevent...
options: Buckling when tracks expand in summer heat
questions.answer: Buckling when tracks expand in summer heat
questions.explanation: Without gaps, expanding rails would push against each other and buckle upward (like a warp) in hot weather.

questions.question: A bimetallic strip bends when heated because...
options: The two metals expand by different amounts
questions.answer: The two metals expand by different amounts
questions.explanation: Two bonded metals with different expansion coefficients: when heated, the one expanding more bends the strip.

questions.question: A bimetallic strip is used in...
questions.explanation: Bimetallic strips act as automatic switches — they bend at set temperature to open/close circuits in thermostats.

questions.question: Water reaches maximum density at...
questions.explanation: Water has maximum density at 4°C. Below 4°C it expands (anomalous). At 0°C it freezes as less-dense ice.

questions.question: Why does ice float on water?
options: Ice is less dense than liquid water
questions.answer: Ice is less dense than liquid water
questions.explanation: Ice forms a crystal structure with more space between molecules → less dense than liquid water → floats.

questions.question: The anomalous expansion of water is important for aquatic life because...
options: insulating water below from extreme cold
questions.answer: Ice floats, insulating water below from extreme cold
questions.explanation: Ice layer on surface insulates water below, keeping it at 4°C and unfrozen → fish and plants survive winter.

questions.question: Linear expansion equation ΔL = αL₀ΔT. If L₀=2m, α=1.2×10⁻⁵/°C, ΔT=50°C, ΔL =
Volume expansion is approximately...
questions.explanation: γ ≈ 3α. Volume expands in three dimensions, so volumetric coefficient ≈ 3× linear coefficient.

questions.question: When setting rivets, the hot rivet is hammered flat and then...
options: contracting to tightly clamp the metal plates
questions.answer: Cooled, contracting to tightly clamp the metal plates
questions.explanation: Hot metal is soft and shaped, then cools and contracts — pulling the joint tighter and clamping the plates firmly.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?

questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Magnetism. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Magnetism concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Magnetism builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Magnetism. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Magnetism. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Magnetism include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Magnetism. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
